statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
A string $ w $ consisting of lowercase Latin letters is called suspicious if and only if all of the following conditions hold:

- The letter $ \mathtt{s} $ appears at least twice, and
- For every occurrence of the letter $ \mathtt{u} $ , the two nearest occurrences of $ \mathtt{s} $ are the same number of characters away from the $ \mathtt{u} $ .

After watching you finish a string task, your friend Aka has gifted you a string $ r $ consisting only of letters $ \mathtt{s} $ and $ \mathtt{u} $ . You can perform the following operation on $ r $ :

- Choose an index $ i $ ( $ 1\le i\le |r| $ ), and set $ r_i $ to $ \mathtt{s} $ .

Determine the minimum number of operations needed to make $ r $ suspicious. It can be shown that, under the given constraints, it is always possible to transform $ r $ into a suspicious string.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains the string $ r $ ( $ 3\le |r|\le 2\cdot 10^5 $ ). It is guaranteed that $ r_i = \mathtt{s} $ or $ \mathtt{u} $ .

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ |r| $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 2\cdot 10 ^ 5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the minimum number of operations needed to make $ r $ suspicious.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
9
sus
uuuu
sssss
uusuuu
suuuuuu
usssssss
sssuuusss
susuusuuus
uuuuuuuuuuu
Output
0
3
0
3
3
1
1
2
6

Note
In the first test case, the string $ \mathtt{sus} $ is already suspicious because $ \mathtt{s} $ appears twice in the string and the two nearest $ \mathtt{s} $ to the only $ \mathtt{u} $ are both $ 1 $ character away: $ \color{red}{\mathtt{s}}\underline{\mathtt{u}}\color{red}{\mathtt{s}} $ .

In the second test case, it is optimal to perform the operation on indices $ 1 $ , $ 3 $ , and $ 4 $ . After that, the string $ s $ becomes $ \texttt{suss} $ . The string $ \mathtt{suss} $ is suspicious because $ \mathtt{s} $ appears $ 3 $ times in the string and the two nearest $ \mathtt{s} $ to the only $ \mathtt{u} $ are both $ 1 $ character away: $ \color{red}{\mathtt{s}}\underline{\mathtt{u}}\color{red}{\mathtt{s}}\mathtt{s} $ .

In the third test case, the condition on $ \mathtt{u} $ is vacuously true because there is no $ \mathtt{u} $ in the string $ \mathtt{sssss} $ . Thus, the given string is already suspicious.

In the sixth test case, the initial string $ \mathtt{usssssss} $ is not suspicious because the two nearest $ \mathtt{s} $ to the only $ \mathtt{u} $ are one and two characters away, respectively: $ \underline{\mathtt{u}}\color{red}{\mathtt{ss}}\mathtt{sssss} $ .

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content:
You have a suspicious string. For a fixed u, suppose the closest s is two characters to the right. Where must the other s be?
Answer 1
It must be two characters to the left. In particular, the two s's must be on opposite sides of the u.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content:
You have a suspicious string. Suppose there are two consecutive u's. Let the closest s to the left u is three characters to the left. Where must the other s be? Where must the closest s's to the right u be?
Answer 2
The s must be three characters to the right, which is two characters to the right of the right u. However, this implies that there must be a corresponding s two characters to the left, which would be the closest s of the left u. By this reasoning, there cannot be two consecutive u's.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2178B - Impost or Sus
Consider a single $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ at index $$$i$$$ in a suspicious string $$$w$$$. The two closest $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ have some distance $$$d$$$ from the $$$\mathtt{u}$$$, and only $$$w_{u-d}$$$ and $$$w_{u+d}$$$ have distance $$$d$$$ from $$$w_i$$$. Therefore, the two closest $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ must be on opposite sides of the $$$\mathtt{u}$$$. This means that no $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ can be on either end of a suspicious string, so a given $$$w_i = \mathtt{u}$$$ must have two neighbors:
If it has a $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ on one side and a $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ on another side, the string is not suspicious — the closest $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ has distance $$$1$$$, but the next closest $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ has distance greater than $$$1$$$.
If it has a $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ on each side, it is in the middle of a larger run of $$$\mathtt{u}$$$'s. This run must eventually end with a $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ that is either on an end of the string, or has a $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ and $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ as its two neighbors. Both are illegal.
If it has a $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ on each side, there is no issue – the two closest $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ have distance $$$1$$$ from the $$$\mathtt{u}$$$.
Therefore, a string is suspicious if and only if:
The first and last characters are $$$\mathtt{s}$$$, and
The neighbors of each $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ are $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ — i.e., no two $$$\mathtt{u}$$$'s are adjacent.
To make a string $$$r$$$ suspicious, we can then do the following:
If $$$r_1 = \mathtt{u}$$$, set it to $$$\mathtt{s}$$$. Repeat for $$$r_n$$$ (where $$$n$$$ is the length of $$$r$$$).
Iterate left to right through the string (increasing order in the index $$$i$$$); if, taking past operations into account, $$$r_i = r_{i-1} = \mathtt{u}$$$, then set $$$r_i = \mathtt{s}$$$.
This greedy algorithm makes the string suspicious; it remains to show that this minimizes the number of operations.
The first step is clearly required and thus optimal. To see that the second step is optimal, consider taking a run of $$$\mathtt{u}$$$'s and pairing up adjacent $$$\mathtt{u}$$$'s from left to right — e.g., $$$\mathtt{s} \ \color{red}{\underline{\mathtt{uu}} \ \underline{\mathtt{uu}} \ \mathtt{u}} \ \mathtt{s}$$$. In each pair, at least one $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ must be changed to an $$$\mathtt{s}$$$ in order to satisfy the condition that no two $$$\mathtt{u}$$$'s are adjacent. The above algorithm changes exactly the second $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ in each pair, so it achieves this lower bound. (Specifically, for each run of length $$$k$$$, it performs $$$\lfloor k/2 \rfloor$$$ operations.)
Running the algorithm and counting the number of operations it performs solves this problem in $$$O(n)$$$.
Optionally, to make the below implementations cleaner, we prepend and append a $$$\mathtt{u}$$$ to the string, then skip the first step of the above algorithm and perform the second step only. This results in the same number of operations. (Why?)
